tools.description: Execute read-only DuckDB query against MotherDuck databases. For cross-database queries, use fully qualified names: database.schema.table (or database.table for main schema).
tools.name: query

tools.description: List all databases in your MotherDuck account with their names and types.
tools.name: list_databases

tools.description: List all tables and views in a MotherDuck database with their comments.

database.description: Database name to list tables from

schema.description: Schema name (optional, defaults to all schemas)

tools.name: list_tables

tools.description: List all columns of a table or view with their types and comments.
tools.name: list_columns

tools.description: Search the catalog for databases, schemas, tables, columns, and shares using fuzzy matching. Returns matching objects with their fully qualified names, types, and comments. Useful for discovering available data when you don't know exact names.

query.description: Search term to find in object names (supports partial matching, underscores, dots)

object_types.description: Optional filter to limit results to specific object types

tools.name: search_catalog

tools.description: Ask a question about DuckDB or MotherDuck. Returns answers from official documentation.
tools.name: ask_docs_question

tools.description: List all database shares that have been shared with you. Returns share names and URLs.
tools.name: list_shares

tools.description: List all dives in MotherDuck. Dives are interactive React data apps that query live data. Returns metadata including current_version (the latest version number, 1-indexed) for each dive. Use read_dive with the optional version parameter to retrieve a specific historical version. Optionally filter by keywords to search in title and description.

keywords.description: Optional keywords to filter dives by title or description (case-insensitive, all words must match)

tools.name: list_dives

tools.description: Save a new dive to MotherDuck. Returns a URL the user can click to view the dive. Call get_dive_guide first and iterate with the user on the design thoroughly before suggesting to save. Never save without previous confirmation that iteration is done.

description.description: A brief description of the dive

content.description: The JSX/React component code for the dive

tools.name: save_dive

tools.description: Update an existing dive's title, description, or content. Returns a URL the user can click to view the updated dive. At least one field must be provided.

id.description: The unique identifier (UUID) of the dive to update

tools.name: update_dive

tools.description: Read a specific dive by ID, including its full JSX/React component code. Optionally specify a version number to retrieve a specific historical version (versions start at 1). If no version is specified, the latest version is returned.

id.description: The unique identifier of the dive to read

version.description: Optional version number to retrieve (1-indexed). If not specified, returns the latest version.

tools.name: read_dive

tools.description: Delete a dive by ID. This action is permanent and cannot be undone.

id.description: The unique identifier of the dive to delete

tools.name: delete_dive

tools.description: Load instructions for creating MotherDuck dives. Call this before starting to iterate with the user on artifacts, data visualizations, plots, dashboards, etc.

client.description: The client environment requesting the guide. Use 'claude' for Claude web/desktop, 'chatgpt' for ChatGPT, 'claude_code' for Cursor/IDE integrations with local filesystem access, or 'other' for other MCP clients.

tools.name: get_dive_guide

tools.description: Share the data for a dive with your organization. Creates org-scoped shares for owned databases used in the dive, and updates the dive so others in the organization can view it.

diveId.description: The unique identifier (UUID) of the dive

tools.name: share_dive_data

summary.text:
**Considering metrics for analysis**

I need to formulate a general answer since I can't access data without an MD database. I'll ask for clarification on which metric and timeframe the user is interested in. It’s important to include frameworks and potentially mention countries like China, Germany, Japan, the US, Korea, and India. I want to ensure a balanced analysis using various indicators like production, exports, and innovation. The conclusion will depend on the criteria provided. I can offer to compute using specific datasets if they share them with me!

content.text:
“Best” depends on what you mean by *doing well* in automotive. Different countries lead on different scorecards (volume, exports, profit/brands, EV transition, innovation). Below is a practical comparison and then a clear “winner” **by segment**—because there isn’t one single nation that dominates every dimension.

## Quick comparison (who leads at what)

### 1) **Scale & momentum (production + growth) → China**
- **Why it’s strong:** China is the largest auto market and one of the largest producers, with the fastest growth in **EVs** and increasingly competitive **exports**.
- **Structural advantages:** massive domestic demand, deep battery supply chain, rapid product cycles, strong cost competitiveness, and policy support.
- **Where it’s weaker:** global brand prestige in ICE/performance is still catching up; geopolitical/trade barriers add risk.

### 2) **Profitability + global brands (value captured per vehicle) → Germany**
- **Why it’s strong:** Germany’s OEMs (premium/luxury) tend to capture high margins and strong pricing power globally.
- **Structural advantages:** engineering depth, premium branding, high-end manufacturing, resilient export base.
- **Where it’s weaker:** EV transition speed and software/platform execution have been more uneven than China’s leaders; higher cost base.

### 3) **Quality, reliability, and manufacturing excellence (especially hybrids) → Japan**
- **Why it’s strong:** exceptional manufacturing systems, reliability reputation, and leadership in **hybrids** with strong global footprints.
- **Structural advantages:** mature supplier ecosystem, disciplined production, strong global operations.
- **Where it’s weaker:** slower full-EV adoption historically (though improving), battery supply chain less dominant than China’s.

### 4) **Technology ecosystem (software/AV + capital markets) → United States**
- **Why it’s strong:** leadership in auto software/compute, autonomy R&D, and EV pioneers; huge market with access to capital.
- **Structural advantages:** tech stack strengths, venture ecosystem, high-end pickup/SUV profit pools.
- **Where it’s weaker:** manufacturing depth across the full supply chain vs. East Asia; inconsistent execution across legacy OEMs; supplier gaps (e.g., some battery materials).

### 5) **Efficient global manufacturing & batteries (fast followers) → South Korea**
- **Why it’s strong:** strong OEMs and world-class battery players; effective scaling and export competitiveness.
- **Where it’s weaker:** smaller home market than China/US; less premium pricing power than Germany.

### 6) **Emerging growth + low-cost manufacturing potential → India**
- **Why it’s strong:** fast-growing domestic market and cost advantages; increasing supplier capability.
- **Where it’s weaker:** still building depth in advanced electronics, batteries, and high-value exports.

---

## So who is “doing the best”?
It depends on the definition:

- **Best in EVs and future volume leadership:** **China**  
  *Because it combines scale, battery/supply-chain dominance, fast iteration, and growing export strength.*

- **Best at profit and premium brand power today:** **Germany**  
  *Because it captures a large share of global automotive profits via premium positioning and engineering brand equity.*

- **Best at dependable mass-market execution (especially hybrids):** **Japan**  
  *Because its manufacturing systems and reliability reputation remain unmatched across many segments.*

If you force a single overall pick for “automotive segment” **right now**, most analysts would choose **China** as the strongest *strategic position* (especially due to EVs and supply chain), while **Germany** remains strongest in *premium profit capture*.

---

## If you tell me your criteria, I can give one definitive winner
Answer these and I’ll rank nations accordingly (with a clear “#1”):
1) Timeframe: **last year**, **5 years**, or **next 5 years**?  
2) Metric priority: **production**, **exports**, **profit**, **EV share**, **innovation/patents**, **jobs**, or **supply-chain control**?  
3) Vehicle focus: **ICE**, **hybrid**, **EV**, **commercial**, or **luxury**?

If you want, I can also build a simple scoring model (weights across metrics) and produce a ranked table.
